{{Short description|Principle that mass can be converted to energy and vice versa}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Mass–energy equivalence
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Special relativity]], [[Energy]], [[Mass]], [[Speed of light]], [[Momentum]]
| formula = <math>E = mc^2</math>
| proposed_by = [[Albert Einstein]], 1905
}}

In [[physics]], '''mass–energy equivalence''' is the principle that [[mass]] is a form of [[energy]], and that mass and energy are interconvertible. This relationship was derived by [[Albert Einstein]] from his theory of [[special relativity]] and is encapsulated in the famous equation:

:<math>E = mc^2</math>

where <math>E</math> is the [[energy]], <math>m</math> is the [[mass]], and <math>c</math> is the [[speed of light]] in vacuum (299,792,458 m/s).

== Meaning ==
The equation <math>E = mc^2</math> means that a small amount of mass is equivalent to an enormous amount of energy. For example, the complete conversion of 1 kg of mass would release approximately 9×10¹⁶ joules — equivalent to about 21 megatons of TNT.

== Derivation ==
From relativistic [[kinetic energy]]:
:<math>E_{\text{kin}} = (\gamma - 1) m_0 c^2</math>

The total energy is:
:<math>E = \gamma m_0 c^2 = m_{\text{rel}} c^2</math>

At rest (<math>v = 0, \gamma = 1</math>):
:<math>E_0 = m_0 c^2</math> — the rest energy

== Relativistic energy–momentum relation ==
The complete relationship between energy, momentum, and mass:
:<math>E^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_0 c^2)^2</math>

For massless particles (e.g., [[photon]]s, <math>m_0 = 0</math>):
:<math>E = pc</math>

== Practical manifestations ==
* '''[[Nuclear fission]]''' — ~0.1% of mass converted to energy
* '''[[Nuclear fusion]]''' — ~0.7% of mass converted to energy
* '''[[Annihilation]]''' — 100% conversion when matter meets [[antimatter]]
* '''[[Binding energy]] in nuclei''' — mass defect explains nuclear stability
* '''[[Pair production]]''' — energy creates particle–antiparticle pairs

== Applications ==
* [[Nuclear power]] — energy from mass defect in fission/fusion
* [[Nuclear weapon]]s — rapid mass–energy conversion
* [[Particle accelerator]]s — creation of massive particles from kinetic energy
* [[PET scan]]s — detection of annihilation photons in medical imaging
* [[Stellar nucleosynthesis]] — mass–energy conversion powers stars

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Energy]]
* [[Mass]]
* [[Momentum]]
* [[Nuclear fission]]
* [[Nuclear fusion]]
* [[Special relativity]]
* [[Speed of light]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Special relativity]]
[[Category:Energy]]
[[Category:Mass]]
[[Category:Albert Einstein]]
[[Category:Equations of physics]]
